title: Fragmented Europe: Dealing with China as a technology and innovation power
gist: MERICS and the European Think-tank Network on China (ETNC) release a 2026 report finding that the EU's declared 'de-risking' shift on China science-and-tech cooperation lacks cohesive implementation, with 24 national chapters plus an EU chapter showing a patchwork of competing member-state approaches to cooperation, competition and rivalry with China.

insights.claim: The EU has shifted toward a policy of 'de-risking' in science-and-technology cooperation with China amid growing economic- and research-security concerns, but this ambition lacks cohesive implementation, leaving a patchwork of sometimes-competing interests and approaches across member states.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: The core investable takeaway: EU-wide China tech policy is not a single coherent regime but 27 diverging national ones, so company-level exposure to China restrictions must be assessed country-by-country rather than assuming uniform EU rules.
insights.quote: this ambition lacks cohesive implementation, and the current state of affairs is a patchwork of sometimes competing interests and approaches across member states
insights.locator: Recent years have seen the EU shift toward a policy of “de-risking”

insights.claim: The 2026 European Think-tank Network on China (ETNC) report canvasses national approaches to China as a technology and research partner across 24 countries plus a dedicated EU chapter, each written by a China expert assessing their own country's relationship with China across cooperation, competition and rivalry dimensions.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: A ready-made 25-country comparative dataset on national China-tech policy stances — useful as a screening tool for which EU jurisdictions are more or less restrictive toward Chinese tech partnerships and investment.
insights.quote: The report features 24 national chapters and a dedicated EU chapter
insights.locator: This year’s report by the European Think Tank Network on China

insights.claim: Each national chapter follows a common analytical structure: recent trends in science-and-technology collaboration with China, the focus areas where China interaction is most relevant, national policy adjustments, and an outlook on how the relationship may evolve — enabling direct cross-country comparison.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: The shared four-part framework (trends, focus areas, policy adjustments, outlook) is a reusable template for tracking how individual EU states' China-tech stance shifts year over year.
insights.locator: The authors tackle this question through

insights.claim: MERICS' Italy chapter, authored by Aurelio Insisa, is explicitly framed around biopharma, automotive and telecoms as the sectors most exposed to China's technological power — naming these as Italy's specific vulnerability areas within the wider EU patchwork.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Flags biopharma, automotive and telecoms as the specific Italian sectors to watch for China-related policy or investment restrictions.
insights.quote: Biopharma, automotive and telecoms sectors are facing China’s technological power
insights.locator: Italy: Biopharma, automotive and telecoms sectors

insights.claim: MERICS' Germany chapter, by Claudia Wessling and Bernhard Bartsch, is framed as tracing a shift 'from mutual benefit to existential competition with China,' indicating MERICS assesses the German-China relationship as having moved from a cooperative to an adversarial framing.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Germany, the EU's largest economy and most China-exposed industrial base, is characterized as having crossed into 'existential competition' framing — a notable hardening worth monitoring against German industrial policy and export data.
insights.quote: From mutual benefit to existential competition with China
insights.locator: Germany: From mutual benefit to existential competition

insights.claim: MERICS' Ireland chapter, by Alexander Davey, is framed around 'interlocking factors' shaping Dublin's approach to China in science-tech innovation — a more multi-causal framing than the binary competition language used for Germany, suggesting Ireland's China exposure is less confrontational.
insights.speaker: MERICS
insights.so_what: Signals Ireland sits at the more cooperative end of the EU's fragmented spectrum on China tech policy, relevant for firms weighing Ireland versus Germany as an EU base for China-linked R&D activity.
insights.quote: Interlocking factors shape the approach to China in science-tech innovation
insights.locator: Ireland: Interlocking factors shape the approach
